title: activity2 report draft
body:
# FINS3666 Assessment 1 Activity 2 - Analysis Report Draft

## 3.1 Pricing Model I implemented a real-time Fair Value (iNAV-style) model for NDQ using intraday NQ futures and AUD/USD. First, I cleaned the 5-second market data and restricted NDQ to the CLOB session only, then aligned NDQ, NQ, and FX on exact UTC 5-second buckets. To calibrate equity sensitivity, I used one year of EOD closes for QQQ.N and NQc1 and estimated a daily OLS beta from overlapping return dates. The estimated beta to NQ was 0.9576. I set FX sensitivity to -1.0 to reflect AUD/USD conversion effects.

Fair Value was anchored at the first valid CLOB observation and updated at each interval using log-return changes in NQ and FX. The model tracked NDQ mid-price closely: MAE 0.0042, RMSE 0.0054, correlation 0.9988, and regression R2 0.9976. These results indicate that cross-market information from futures and FX can explain much of intraday NDQ price movement. In practice, the mispricing series can be interpreted as a short-horizon premium or discount around the model fair value, which is useful for market-making monitoring and for checking whether observed quotes are consistent with related-market moves.

## 3.2 Spread Model I modeled quoted spread in basis points with an empirical regression on microstructure features: rolling volatility (NDQ, NQ, FX), inverse depth, absolute imbalance, absolute mispricing, and 1-minute trade intensity. The model used chronological train/test splitting (70/30) to avoid look-ahead bias. Predicted spreads were floored at the minimum positive training spread to preserve non-negativity.

Out-of-sample performance was MAE 0.9370, RMSE 1.0529, and R2 0.1842, compared with a train-mean baseline MAE 1.0962 and RMSE 1.2105. The strongest absolute coefficients were: rv_ndq (3409.3454), rv_nq (2872.7788), abs_mispricing (1701.9675). I then converted predicted spread bps into price spreads and generated model-implied bid/ask quotes around Fair Value. Because the model beat the constant-spread baseline in error terms, it provides evidence that intraday spread variation is partly systematic and linked to changing volatility, depth, and order-flow conditions. The model-implied bid and ask also provide a consistent bridge between valuation and execution costs.

## 3.3 Critical Reflection Strengths: the workflow is fully reproducible, uses transparent assumptions, and combines valuation and liquidity modeling in one framework. Exact 5-second alignment and strict session filtering reduce noise from auctions and off-session behavior.

Limitations: the pricing model uses fixed FX elasticity and linear sensitivity to NQ, while spread dynamics may be nonlinear and state-dependent. The spread model is still a reduced-form approximation and does not explicitly model inventory risk or adverse-selection intensity.

For real-world use, improvements include dynamic beta estimation, regime-switching volatility features, richer order-book variables, and inventory-aware quoting methods such as Avellaneda-Stoikov style control. These extensions can better capture changing market conditions and execution risk in market making. Additional practical enhancements include venue-specific transaction cost inputs, event-time seasonality adjustments, and stress testing during sharp macro moves where both futures and FX can gap. Integrating these elements would improve robustness and reduce model risk when deploying automated pricing rules.
